# Introduction: a quantitative stance {#sec-intro}

## The problem we begin with

Consider a multi-centre clinical trial run by five
hospitals. Each site enrols participants, assigns them to
treatment through a shared randomisation scheme, and
records outcome events. Every site keeps a copy of the
growing record, and a regulator will one day audit it.
Now suppose that no site is willing to let any other site
act as sole custodian of that record. Perhaps the sponsor
has a financial interest in the outcome; perhaps one
site's data systems have been compromised before; perhaps
the institutions are simply in different countries under
different legal regimes. The question is this: can the
five sites maintain a single, shared, append-only record
of trial events, agreed by all, alterable by none, with
no referee?

This is not a hypothetical framing invented to make a
cryptocurrency course palatable to a public-health
audience. It is, we shall argue, the same mathematical
problem that Bitcoin solved in 2008, stripped of its
monetary costume. A blockchain is an append-only ledger
that mutually distrusting parties agree on without a
trusted intermediary. Every mechanism this book studies
exists to make some part of that sentence work:
cryptographic hashing makes the ledger tamper-evident,
digital signatures make entries attributable, and a
consensus protocol makes the parties agree on a single
order of entries despite having no reason to trust one
another. The remarkable fact, established by
@nakamoto2008bitcoin, is that this is possible at all in
a setting where anyone may join, leave, or lie.

We shall study these systems the way a statistician would
want them studied: as objects of quantitative analysis
rather than objects of belief. The central pedagogical
claim of the book is that the reader already owns most of
the hard ideas, in another vocabulary. The probability
that an attacker rewrites confirmed history is the tail
of a random walk. The ten-minute block interval is the
set point of a feedback controller acting on a Poisson
process. Validator selection in proof-of-stake is
probability-proportional-to-size sampling. Wealth
concentration on a chain is a Lorenz curve. Our work,
chapter by chapter, is to perform these translations and
then to use the reader's existing fluency to reach
conclusions that most accounts of this subject never make
precise.

## Why the public-health reader has the advantage

Writing on blockchain divides into two genres that both
fail a quantitative reader. The developer tutorial
teaches how to call an interface without explaining why
the system is built as it is. The promotional explainer
supplies confidence in place of understanding. Neither
serves a reader who can fit a survival model, design a
sampling plan, or evaluate a forecast.

The reader of this book can do something most
participants in this field cannot: attach a number to a
claim and check it. That capacity is the single most
valuable instrument for navigating a domain in which the
median public statement is promotional. A good deal of
the course consists of taking an assertion that
circulates as folklore, writing down the model it
presupposes, and computing whether the assertion is true.
The reader will find the experience familiar; it is the
same discipline one applies to a claimed treatment effect
or a surprising surveillance signal.

## Why now: the technology is arriving in health

We should say plainly why a health scientist ought to
spend this effort now rather than leave the subject to
the engineers. The reason is that the verifiable-data
machinery has stopped being hypothetical in health and
has become infrastructure. Cryptographically signed
health credentials reached hundreds of millions of people
during the pandemic through the SMART Health Cards
standard [@vci2024smart], and the World Health
Organization now operates a global trust network for
verifying such credentials across borders [@who2023gdhcn].
Prescription-drug supply chains in Europe and the United
States are being made traceable to the individual package
by regulation, with at least one production ledger
[@fda2023dscsa] serving that mandate, and a national
health system, Estonia's, has for years anchored the
integrity of its records to a hash-linked timestamping
service [@guardtime2016estonia]. As predictive artificial
intelligence enters clinical care, regulators have begun
to demand a tamper-evident account of the data and models
behind a recommendation [@onc2024hti1].

The instructive part is that most of these successes are
built from cryptographic signatures and hashes rather than
from tokens and mining, and that the ventures which
insisted on the latter have largely failed. The health
professional of the next decade will be asked, repeatedly,
to judge such systems: to say whether a vendor's claim is
a protocol fact or a narrative, whether a distributed
ledger earns its cost over a well-kept database, whether
an attestation proves what it purports to. That judgement,
not the ability to implement a chain, is the competence
this book builds, and it is the reason the subject belongs
in a public-health education now.

## The falsifiability discipline

We maintain a critical lens by construction, not as an
attitude. The foundational cryptography solved a genuine
and difficult problem, namely open-membership Byzantine
agreement without a trusted party [@lamport1982byzantine].
A great deal of what has been built on that foundation is
speculation, and some is fraud. Both statements are true,
and a calibrated analyst holds both without collapsing
into either evangelism or dismissal.

To keep the analysis disciplined, we classify every claim
we encounter into one of four tiers, and we demote each
assertion to its true tier before believing it.

::: {.callout-important}
## The four falsifiability tiers

1. **Protocol fact.** Verifiable by reading code, running a
   node, or checking a proof. Trust after verification.
2. **Empirical claim.** A measurable statement about
   observed on-chain or market data. Demand the data and
   the method.
3. **Economic claim.** A conditional statement about
   incentives that holds under stated assumptions. Ask what
   the assumptions are and whether they hold.
4. **Narrative.** An unfalsifiable or forward-looking story
   about value or destiny. Treat as marketing until it is
   operationalised into a tier-two or tier-three claim.
:::

The field's characteristic failure is tier-slippage: a
narrative delivered in the confident cadence of a
protocol fact. Naming the tier is the antidote, and we
shall ask the reader to do it reflexively. The
epidemiologist will recognise the exercise; it is the
same habit one brings to a causal claim made from an
observational study.

## The running case study

One application threads the entire book, so that the
reader sees a single realistic system developed against
every layer of the stack.

- **The trial ledger.** The multi-institution
  clinical-trial data-integrity ledger with which we
  opened: several hospitals, a shared append-only record
  of enrolment, randomisation, and outcome events,
  tamper-evident, attributable, and auditable by a
  regulator after the fact, with no single party able to
  rewrite history. Every chapter returns to it.
- **The cold chain.** A vaccine cold-chain provenance
  system, our secondary example: verifying that each vial
  was kept within temperature bounds from manufacture to
  administration, across custodians with no common
  authority.

As each mechanism appears (hashing, Merkle trees,
consensus, the account model, smart contracts, on-chain
analysis) we shall ask what it contributes to these two
systems, and whether it is the right tool. We should say
at the outset that the answer is frequently no.

::: {.callout-warning}
## When not to use a blockchain

A blockchain buys you trust-minimised agreement among
parties with no common authority, at a large cost in
throughput, latency, and complexity. If the parties already
trust a custodian, or one could be appointed, a replicated
database with cryptographic audit logging delivers
tamper-evidence without the consensus overhead. Most
'blockchain for health records' proposals fail this test.
The book teaches you to apply it.
:::

## A first computation

The atomic operation of the entire field is the
cryptographic hash: a deterministic function that maps
any input to a fixed-length output and behaves, for all
practical purposes, as if it were random. We shall study
its statistical properties in @sec-crypto. For now, we
invite the reader to observe the two properties that make
a ledger tamper-evident: the same input always yields the
same output (determinism), and a one-character change
yields an unrecognisably different output (the avalanche
effect).

```{r intro-hash}
library(digest)
h <- function(x) {
  digest(x, algo = 'sha256', serialize = FALSE)
}

h('trial-record: patient 001, arm A, enrolled 2026-02-01')
h('trial-record: patient 001, arm B, enrolled 2026-02-01')
```

The two records differ in a single character, the
treatment arm, yet their hashes share no visible
structure. If the trial ledger stored the hash of each
record, altering any record after the fact would change
its hash and betray the tampering. That is the whole idea
of a tamper-evident log. Everything else in the book is,
in a sense we shall make precise, an elaboration of this
one computation.

## The ten-week map

The book is a ten-week course in four parts, one chapter
per teaching week.

- **Part I, Foundations.** The consensus problem in the
  permissionless setting (@sec-consensus), and the
  cryptographic primitives studied empirically in R
  (@sec-crypto).
- **Part II, Bitcoin and Nakamoto consensus.**
  Proof-of-work and the backbone protocol (@sec-pow); the
  statistics of mining as a renewal and control system
  (@sec-mining); and the security economics of attacks
  (@sec-security).
- **Part III, Ethereum and programmable state.** The
  account model and the virtual machine (@sec-ethereum);
  decentralised finance as applied mathematics
  (@sec-defi); and maximal extractable value as market
  microstructure (@sec-mev).
- **Part IV, Proof-of-stake and on-chain data.**
  Stake-based consensus, finality, and restaking risk
  (@sec-pos); and the statistical analysis of on-chain
  data, including tokenomics and wealth concentration
  (@sec-onchain).

## How to use this book

In closing, three habits are to be emphasized. First,
read the primary source before the media. For each week
the Further reading section names the whitepaper,
specification, or paper that is the ground truth; read it
first, so that no promotional account can install a
misconception before you have seen the real thing.
Second, run the code. Every computational result in the
book is generated at build time from the R shown beside
it, and re-running it is the fastest way to convert
reading into understanding. Third, keep a calibrated
forecasting record, introduced in @sec-onchain and scored
by the Brier score, so that your scepticism is itself
held to account rather than merely asserted.

The reference textbook throughout is
@narayanan2016bitcoin, whose rigour set the standard this
book tries to meet for a public-health audience.
